result: The world is full of small, unnoticed miracles. A spider weaves its web in the shadow of a bus stop, each silken strand a tiny suspension bridge of intent. A child learns the word “butterfly,” and suddenly every garden becomes a theater of color and flight. Meanwhile, galaxies collide in silence, birthing stars that no human celebration will ever toast. I think about that imbalance—how momentous events thunder on without applause, while we obsess over typos in yesterday’s e-mail. It comforts me, like being tucked under an indifferent sky: the pressure to matter dissolves when you realize nothing and everything matters equally. Yesterday I watched a man rescue an earthworm from hot pavement, lifting it with a leaf so delicate the worm’s segments still pulsed like miniature accordions. He didn’t tweet it, didn’t wait for validation; he just set it on the grass and kept walking. That anonymity felt holy. We’re taught to scale our lives—more followers, more zeros, more square footage—yet the inverse is where peace hides: smaller, slower, unseen. I want to live there, curled inside the parentheses of ordinary afternoons, where tea cools at the speed of whispered secrets and the refrigerator hums in B-flat. I want to write sentences that nobody will underline, plant trees whose names I can’t pronounce, and mispronounce them with joy. If meaning exists, it’s probably spelled wrong, smudged by thumbprints, stuck to the bottom of my shoe like chewing gum infused with gravel. And still it sparkles when the light hits just right.

A child learns the word “butterfly,” and suddenly every garden becomes a theater of color and flight. Meanwhile, galaxies collide in silence, birthing stars that no human celebration will ever toast. I think about that imbalance—how momentous events thunder on without applause, while we obsess over typos in yesterday’s e-mail. It comforts me, like being tucked under an indifferent sky: the pressure to matter dissolves when you realize nothing and everything matters equally. Yesterday I watched a man rescue an earthworm from hot pavement, lifting it with a leaf so delicate the worm’s segments still pulsed like miniature accordions. He didn’t tweet it, didn’t wait for validation; he just set it on the grass and kept walking. That anonymity felt holy. We’re taught to scale our lives—more followers, more zeros, more square footage—yet the inverse is where peace hides: smaller, slower, unseen. I want to live there, curled inside the parentheses of ordinary afternoons, where tea cools at the speed of whispered secrets and the refrigerator hums in B-flat. I want to write sentences that nobody will underline, plant trees whose names I can’t pronounce, and mispronounce them with joy. If meaning exists, it’s probably spelled wrong, smudged by thumbprints, stuck to the bottom of my shoe like chewing gum infused with gravel. And still it sparkles when the light hits just right.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
